name: zig-best-practices
description: Provides Zig patterns for type-first development with tagged unions, explicit error sets, comptime validation, and memory management. Must use when reading or writing Zig files.
capabilities: Guide type-first development with tagged unions and explicit error sets for compile-time safety
Provide memory management patterns using explicit allocators, defer, and errdefer cleanup
Show comptime validation techniques using @compileError and type constraints
Demonstrate C interop patterns with @cImport and extern functions for system programming
Explain generic data structure implementation using type-returning functions and comptime
Teach debugging patterns with GeneralPurposeAllocator for memory leak detection
author: 0xBigBoss
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown files with Zig programming guidance. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or external command execution. All 212 static findings are false positives triggered by documentation patterns: backticks in markdown code blocks, Zig's @import() compile-time mechanism, and legitimate programming terminology. Content is educational and poses no security risk.
valueStatement: Writing safe, efficient Zig code requires understanding type-first development patterns and explicit memory management. This skill provides proven patterns for tagged unions, error sets, comptime validation, and resource cleanup that produce robust, maintainable Zig applications.
limitations: Provides patterns and guidance only; does not execute or compile Zig code
Does not replace reading Zig documentation for complete API reference
Does not configure build systems or manage Zig toolchain installation

useCases.title: Safe error handling
useCases.description: Define explicit error sets and use error unions to make failure modes visible and handled at compile time.

useCases.title: Memory-safe patterns
useCases.description: Implement proper resource cleanup with defer and errdefer to prevent memory leaks and use-after-free bugs.

useCases.title: Generic containers
useCases.description: Build type-safe generic data structures like queues and stacks using comptime type parameters.

promptTemplates.title: Error set definition
promptTemplates.prompt: Write an explicit error set for parsing JSON and show how to use it with error unions.

promptTemplates.title: Memory cleanup
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a function that allocates a resource and uses errdefer to clean up on error paths.

promptTemplates.title: Tagged union state machine
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement a request state machine using tagged unions where only valid state combinations are possible.

promptTemplates.title: Comptime validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a generic buffer struct that validates its size parameter at compile time using @compileError.

bestPractices: Define types before implementation; let the compiler verify completeness through exhaustive handling of all cases
Pass allocators explicitly to functions rather than using global state; this makes memory usage explicit and testable
Use errdefer immediately after resource acquisition to ensure cleanup runs on any error exit path
antiPatterns: Using anyerror instead of specific error sets hides failure modes and makes error handling incomplete
Skipping else clauses in switch statements allows unhandled cases to slip through silently
Using defer for conditional cleanup instead of errdefer causes resource leaks when errors occur

faq.question: Does this skill work with the latest Zig version?
faq.answer: Yes. Patterns follow Zig 0.15+ conventions. Minor syntax changes may occur between versions.

faq.question: What memory allocators should I use in production?
faq.answer: Use GeneralPurposeAllocator for debugging. Switch to page_allocator or arena allocators in release builds.

faq.question: How does this skill integrate with Claude Code?
faq.answer: The skill loads automatically when you work with Zig files. It guides the AI to apply idiomatic patterns.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill contains only documentation. It does not access files, network, or execute code.

faq.question: Why does the skill recommend explicit error sets?
faq.answer: Explicit error sets document exactly what can fail. This enables exhaustive handling and prevents ignored errors.

faq.question: How does this compare to Rust or C for safety?
faq.answer: Zig takes a different approach. Instead of lifetimes, it uses explicit allocator parameters and defer cleanup.
